goal: README rewrite for trial: explicit who-runs-what audience split + Cursor walkthrough + pain-table

summary.title: README rewrite for trial: who-runs-what + Cursor walkthrough + pain table
summary.what: Replace the README opening (lines 1-65 of the prior version) with four new sections aimed at trial users who don't yet know how Mainline fits their workflow. (1) Two-paragraph opener with the canonical positioning line plus a concrete pain frame. (2) `Who runs what?` — two side-by-side command groups for the agent loop vs the human loop. (3) `If you use Cursor (or Claude Code / Codex)` — concrete hooks-install walkthrough explaining what session-start injects, plus the no-hooks fallback. (4) 5-row pain/benefit table grounding the value before any architecture diagram. The 5-minute quickstart is rewritten with `[you]` vs `[agent]` annotations on every line and mentions the seal_result_starter field shipped in PR #73. Architecture / Concepts / Daily commands / Hooks / FAQ sections unchanged.
summary.why: Live trial feedback while shipping yesterday's polish: 'users have no idea how to use it, themselves or via the agent. They use Cursor — how does this fit?'. The old README led with phase-1/phase-2 conflict-detection theory and never told readers which commands they personally type vs which the agent runs. Trial users tomorrow need that distinction front-and-center, with a Cursor-specific walkthrough they can follow in 60 seconds.
summary.user_goal: A Cursor user reads the first three sections of README in two minutes and understands: what Mainline does for them, what they personally type vs what the agent runs, and how to wire it into Cursor in one command.

decisions.point: lead with audience split, not theory
decisions.chose: `Who runs what?` is the second section (after the one-paragraph opener), before any architecture / concepts.
decisions.rationale: The audit found users were confused about whether they or the agent runs the commands. The split has to be the lead, not buried.

decisions.point: Cursor-specific walkthrough as a named section
decisions.chose: Dedicated 'If you use Cursor (or Claude Code / Codex)' section with a concrete two-line setup.
decisions.rationale: Cursor is the modal trial user's tool. Naming it explicitly tells them 'yes, this is for you'. Including Claude Code / Codex in parentheses keeps the section discoverable for those users without burying Cursor.

decisions.chose: Inline `[you]` / `[agent]` markers on every line of the bash block.
decisions.rationale: Audience-per-line is the smallest possible change that closes the audience-confusion gap inside the quickstart. Alternative was two separate quickstarts, one per audience — but they share most lines, and side-by-side annotation keeps the loop's shape visible.

decisions.chose: Five rows showing the concrete failure mode → Mainline fix.
decisions.rationale: Architecture diagrams are for people who already understand why they need Mainline. The pain table is for people deciding whether they need it.

decisions.chose: Architecture / Concepts / Daily commands / Hooks / FAQ / Storage layout / Development sections unchanged.
decisions.rationale: Those are reference material that's accurate today. The bottleneck for trial users is the first three sections; everything below is good once they're sold.

rejected.alternative: Split the README into two files (one for humans, one for agents).
rejected.reason: Two READMEs would require trial users to figure out which one is for them — exactly the audience-confusion problem we're trying to fix. One README with audience-per-line annotations is clearer.

rejected.alternative: Drop the whole conflict-detection theory and rewrite as a marketing pitch.
rejected.reason: The architecture / concepts content IS the long tail of value for users who buy in. Removing it would make the README read like an ad.

rejected.alternative: Embed a screenshot of Cursor with Mainline-injected context.
rejected.reason: Useful but out of scope for tonight's rewrite; markdown in a text README is plenty to demo the wire-up. Worth a followup.

risks: The pain table claims specific outcomes (e.g. 'agent reads the anti-pattern and stops before the diff'). For trial users whose seals don't yet have anti-patterns recorded, the claim won't hold yet — Mainline can only surface what's been written. The wording 'stop your AI agent from...' implies more enforcement than exists; the table's right column says 'reads the anti-pattern' which is the actual mechanism, but a hasty reader might miss that nuance.

Trial users who don't use a supported hook agent and don't read the no-hooks fallback paragraph might think Mainline only works with Cursor / Claude Code / Codex. Acceptable for tomorrow; the fallback paragraph is one sentence under the Cursor section.

anti_patterns.what: Splitting the README into per-audience files
anti_patterns.why: Reintroduces the audience-confusion problem the rewrite is fixing. One README, audience-per-line annotations.

anti_patterns.what: Removing the architecture / concepts sections to shorten the README
anti_patterns.why: Those sections are the long tail of value for users who buy in. Removing them would help nobody — the trial-user bottleneck is the opening, not the depth.

anti_patterns.what: Letting the README's opening positioning drift from AGENTS.md template
anti_patterns.why: Two front doors with two different one-liners confuse readers who land on each. Same canonical line in both, always.

followups: Add a Cursor screenshot showing the additional_context payload from session-start (visual proof that hooks work).
Audit the FAQ for 'agent says X' style answers that could move into the new audience-split frame.
Once the LLM-runner eval ships, link to results from the README so trial users see the thesis is empirically tested.
Translate the 'Who runs what?' section into Chinese for the user's primary audience — current README is English-only.

architectural_claims: README opens with 'who runs what' before anything else; architecture / concepts come after the audience is oriented.
Cursor / Claude Code / Codex hooks integration is the recommended path for trial users; no-hooks AGENTS.md path is the documented fallback, both tested in CI today.
The 5-minute quickstart is the load-bearing onboarding surface; every line is audience-annotated so trial users can see at a glance which steps they personally type.
behavioral_changes: README opening (≈100 lines) replaced. Architecture and below unchanged.
5-minute quickstart bash block now annotates each line with [you] or [agent] so audience-per-line is unmissable.
